body.label: modulo_36.root.thm
body.claim: If c > 0 and ac is congruent to bc modulo mc, then a is congruent to b modulo m.

proof_steps.assumption: c > 0 and ac is congruent to bc modulo mc

deduced_from_claim: ac is congruent to bc modulo mc

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For integers x,y,n, x is congruent to y modulo n if and only if n divides x-y.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Definition of congruence modulo n

proof_steps.proof_method: By the definition of congruence modulo mc.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For integers d and r, d divides r if and only if there exists an integer k such that r=kd.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Definition of divisibility

proof_steps.proof_method: Rewriting kmc as (km)c from the preceding equality.
Since c is positive, it is nonzero.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: If xc=yc and c≠0, then x=y.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Cancellation law
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to cancel the common factor c.

proof_steps.proof_method: Cancel the nonzero factor c from both sides of (a-b)c=(km)c.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For integers d and r, d divides r if and only if there exists an integer k such that r=kd.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Definition of divisibility

proof_steps.proof_method: Since a-b is an integer multiple of m.

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For integers x,y,n, x is congruent to y modulo n if and only if n divides x-y.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Definition of congruence modulo n

proof_steps.claim: a is congruent to b modulo m
proof_steps.proof_method: By the definition of congruence modulo m.

proof.text:
Assume $c>0$ and that $ac$ is congruent to $bc$ modulo $mc$. By the definition of congruence modulo $mc$, this means that $mc$ divides $ac-bc$. Hence there exists an integer $k$ such that
\[
ac - bc = (a-b)c = kmc.
\]
Thus we have the equality
\[
(a-b)c = (km)c.
\]

Since $c>0$, it follows that $c\neq 0$. We can therefore cancel the factor $c$ from both sides of the equality. This implies
\[
a - b = km.
\]
Hence $m$ divides $a-b$. By the definition of congruence modulo $m$, this means that $a$ is congruent to $b$ modulo $m$.
